commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: 4. What meaneth, "Thou hast charged that we shall keep Thy commandments too much"? (ver. 4). Is it, "Thou hast charged too much"? or, "to keep too much"? Whichever of these we understand, the sense seems contrary to that memorable and noble sentiment which the Greeks praise in their wise men, and which the Latins agree in praising. "Do nothing too much." ...But the Latin language sometimes uses the word nimis in such a sense, that we find it in the holy Scripture, and employ it in our discourses, as signifying, very much. In this passage, "Thou hast charged that we keep Thy commandments too much," we simply understand very much, if we understand rightly; and if we say to any very dear friend, I love you too much, we do not wish to be understood to mean more than is fitting, but very much.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content:
Diligently. Nimis. Literally, "too much. "But this is a Hebrew idiom, to imply the greatest diligence. (Haydock)
Some would refer it to "God's strong injunction "which is not necessary. The psalmist henceforward speaks to God. (Berthier)
